easy.question: What is the stated aim of Midland Heart’s Allocations Policy?
easy.answer: To allocate properties and prioritise applicants fairly, supporting balanced and sustainable communities while considering the needs of existing residents and new applicants. (See §1.1.) :contentReference[oaicite:23]{index=23}

easy.question: How are Midland Heart homes let across partners?
easy.answer: 50% are advertised/let by local councils under their allocations policies; the other 50% are let directly by Midland Heart under this policy. (See §4.1–4.2.) :contentReference[oaicite:24]{index=24}

easy.question: Where are properties advertised and for how long at minimum?
easy.answer: Principally on Midland Heart Homes (website/app); adverts can appear any day/time and run for a minimum of 5 working days. (See §5.3.1–5.3.2.) :contentReference[oaicite:25]{index=25}

easy.question: Who is considered to have housing ‘need’ to apply?
easy.answer: Anyone who cannot afford to rent or buy in the private sector, subject to eligibility and exclusions. (See §5.1 and Appendix A/B/E.) :contentReference[oaicite:26]{index=26}

easy.question: Who is chosen first after an advert closes?
easy.answer: The applicant active with Midland Heart Homes the longest who submitted a complete expression of interest evidencing eligibility and any preference criteria. (See §5.4.1.) :contentReference[oaicite:27]{index=27}

easy.question: What are examples of restrictions on who can apply for a property?
easy.answer: Local Lettings Plans and age-restricted properties; restrictions can be relaxed for ‘hard to let’ homes after two unsuccessful advertising routes. (See §5.2.2–5.2.3.) :contentReference[oaicite:28]{index=28}

easy.question: What proportion of adverts aim to have no preference attached and why?
easy.answer: 40% over a rolling 12 months to help maintain balance and openness of access. (See §5.3.3–5.3.4.) :contentReference[oaicite:29]{index=29}

easy.question: List three circumstances when a direct offer may be made instead of advertising.
easy.answer: Move-on from supported housing; adaptations not reasonable/possible at current home; urgent police-advised safety need; severe overcrowding linked to health/safety (often damp/mould); redevelopment/sale; or after eviction when debt is paid in full. Usually only one offer is made. (See §5.5.) :contentReference[oaicite:30]{index=30}

easy.question: What bedroom standard generally applies?
easy.answer: The DWP bedroom standard, allowing for extra rooms in defined cases (e.g., overnight carer, disability-related need). (See Appendix C ‘Bedroom standard’.) :contentReference[oaicite:31]{index=31}

medium.question: An applicant bids for a flat but cannot upload ID and rent-in-advance evidence before the advert closes. What happens?
medium.answer: They may not be eligible for the offer due to ‘lack of proofs’; required evidence must be provided in the specified time for an expression of interest to proceed. (See §5.3.5 and Appendix B ‘Lack of proofs’.) :contentReference[oaicite:32]{index=32}

medium.question: How do preference groups help rebalance stock for under-occupiers and overcrowded tenants?
medium.answer: Preferences can favour spare-bedroom social tenants for smaller homes and overcrowded tenants for larger homes, freeing stock and improving best use. (See Appendix D ‘Preference Groups’.) :contentReference[oaicite:33]{index=33}

medium.question: Is an EEA citizen without EU Settlement Scheme status after 30 June 2021 eligible?
medium.answer: No, they may fail the Right to Rent check and be ineligible unless they have an alternative immigration status; eligibility must align with immigration law. (See Appendix B ‘Immigration status’.) :contentReference[oaicite:34]{index=34}

medium.question: Summarise how private sector affordability is assessed and when savings exclude an applicant.
medium.answer: Median private rents (West Midlands VOA) are compared to gross income: >27% (single/couple) or >20% (with dependants) = unaffordable. Applicants with >£16,000 in savings and sufficient income are considered able to rent/buy privately and thus ineligible. (See Appendix A.) :contentReference[oaicite:35]{index=35}

medium.question: Can a younger live-in carer be housed in an age-restricted scheme?
medium.answer: Yes—exceptions can allow a different age where a live-in carer (on appropriate benefits) or an adaptation need justifies it. (See §5.2.2 ‘Age restricted properties’.) :contentReference[oaicite:36]{index=36}

medium.question: What does ‘hard to let’ mean and what flexibility does it trigger?
medium.answer: A property (or similar nearby) advertised through two different routes unsuccessfully; restrictions may then be relaxed to secure a let. (See §5.2.3.) :contentReference[oaicite:37]{index=37}

medium.question: When can existing Midland Heart tenants transfer?
medium.answer: Not if adequately housed—unless they evidence additional needs (e.g., over/under-occupation, affordability, proximity to work/education/care, or medical/access needs). (See §5.8.) :contentReference[oaicite:38]{index=38}

medium.question: How can a decision be challenged?
medium.answer: Applicants can request a review by contacting the Customer Hub; a more senior officer reviews. Complaints follow the published complaints policy. (See §5.9.) :contentReference[oaicite:39]{index=39}

hard.question: Two applicants meet eligibility for a popular 2-bed flat. Applicant A has the longest active date but does not meet the advert’s preference; Applicant B meets the preference and provided full evidence on time. Who is offered first and why? Include exceptions.
hard.answer: Applicant B—preferences apply first for the 5-day advert window; the applicant who meets preference and evidences eligibility is prioritised. Exceptions include later findings of ineligibility, failure to meet preferences, non-engagement, or rent-in-advance not met. (See §5.3.3–5.3.5; §5.4.1–5.4.3.) :contentReference[oaicite:40]{index=40}

hard.question: A family faces severe overcrowding leading to health risks from damp/mould. Outline how a direct offer would work, any limits on preferences, and validity.
hard.answer: A direct offer may be made where rehousing is the only solution to immediate and significant H&S risk (often linked to damp/mould). Midland Heart cannot guarantee all preferences but aims for safe, suitable housing; typically one suitable offer; application valid for one month and reviewed thereafter. (See §5.5.) :contentReference[oaicite:41]{index=41}

hard.question: In a mixed-status household, one proposed joint tenant lacks eligibility under immigration rules. What tenancy options exist and how are household members treated for size assessment?
hard.answer: A joint tenancy cannot be granted if any joint applicant is ineligible; a sole tenancy may be granted to the eligible person. Ineligible family members can still be counted when determining property size. (See Appendix B ‘Immigration status’.) :contentReference[oaicite:42]{index=42}

hard.question: An applicant has £900 housing debt to another RP and started a repayment plan three months ago. Can they receive an offer now? What exceptions might apply?
hard.answer: Generally no—debt must be cleared or a plan sustained for at least 6 months with debt below £500 (stricter for internal transfers). Exceptions may be approved for exceptional circumstances (e.g., DV financial control, resolved medical/support issues) by the Lettings Operations Manager; eviction re-offer rules also exist if debt is fully paid within a week of eviction. (See Appendix B ‘Housing related debt’.) :contentReference[oaicite:43]{index=43}

hard.question: Explain discretionary departures from the standard bedroom rules for (a) foster carers, (b) downsizers, and (c) medical equipment/overnight carer scenarios, including affordability checks.
hard.answer: (a) Property size may reflect typical fostering numbers; (b) downsizers may under-occupy if affordable; (c) an extra room can be allowed for disability/overnight carer/equipment—subject to discretionary benefit or affordability checks. (See Appendix C ‘Bedroom standard’ and discretion bullets.) :contentReference[oaicite:44]{index=44}

hard.question: An applicant’s income suggests the private sector is affordable, but they face large, non-housing debt repayments. Can they still be considered and what income is disregarded in the affordability test?
hard.answer: Yes—exceptional hardship may be applied by the Lettings Operations Manager considering individual circumstances. Housing Benefit/UC Housing Element and certain disability/carer benefits are disregarded in the affordability calculation. (See Appendix A ‘Exceptional hardship’ and ‘Disregarded income’.) :contentReference[oaicite:45]{index=45}

hard.question: What consequence can follow repeated refusals of reasonable offers within 12 months and how does this relate to ongoing ‘housing need’?
hard.answer: Accounts may be reviewed and applicants can be unable to rejoin for a year; after successful rehousing, ‘housing need’ is treated as met unless circumstances change, and deliberate worsening can lead to no offer. (See Appendix E end.) :contentReference[oaicite:46]{index=46}
